observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Cause the immediate reconstitution of  the Inventory Committee;
Direct the reconstituted Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of PPEs in accordance with the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability, as well as, to ensure timely submission of the RPCPPE to the Auditor; and
Require the Property Officer to maintain updated PCs to account for any PPE receipt and disposition, and to reconcile the same with the subsidiary ledgers maintained by the Accountant.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exhaust efforts to retrieve the records of the PPEs with no details, and provide the corresponding depreciation expenses, using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to verify the details of the completed projects and immediately record the transfer of the project costs from the Trust Fund to the General Fund books, and provide the corresponding depreciation expenses.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Local Development Council and concerned offices/departments/officials to:
Hasten the implementation and completion of the remaining 2020 non-implemented development projects; and
Thoroughly plan the projects that should be included in the Annual Development Plan/Annual Investment Program and ensure that the same are feasible for implementation within the timeline.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to:
Take up in the appropriate inventory account the cost of supplies, materials and relief goods procured for distribution, and in the corresponding expense account the subsequent issuance/distribution duly supported by the RIS and RSMI;
Record in the books the inventory of welfare goods for distribution in custody of the Municipality based on the inventory report prepared; and
Prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Card for each inventory of welfare/relief goods procured by the Municipality.
Require the Supply/Property Officer to:
Coordinate with the LDRRM Officer to prepare and submit an Inventory List/Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution in custody of the Municipality as of December 31, 2020 in the prescribed format in Annex C; and
Prepare and maintain Stock Cards, Requisition and Issue Slip, and Report of Supplies and Materials Issued for welfare goods.
Cause the submission of the duly validated/approved list of beneficiaries, proof of receipt and distribution of welfare goods to individual beneficiaries/recipients, as well as, the Inventory List/Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Cause the inclusion of a warranty clause  in succeeding Contracts/POs under Emergency Procurement of the Bayanihan Act, to ensure assurance of replacement of/recovery on goods delivered with defects, and/or do not conform to quality standards and technical specifications of the contract; and
Ensure that all Contracts/POs contain complete detailed item specifications pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to comply with the posting requirements in the GPPB Online Portal of all projects under the Bayanihan Act undertaken through Negotiated Procurement – Emergency Cases.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee exert efforts to reconcile the PPE accounts and draw journal entry voucher (JEV) to adjust the accounts affected.
observations.reason:
Finding first covered in 2015 AAR

Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide the Appraisal Committee with the list of PPEs acquired in 2002 and earlier so that the latter could appraise and determine the fair market value and estimated remaining useful lives. The Municipal Accountant shall then compute the depreciation expenses using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method and record the same in the books.
observations.reason:
Finding first covered in 2013 AAR

Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 2

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the transfer of the cost of completed projects booked as PPE under the Trust Fund to the General Fund. Corresponding depreciation expenses shall then be provided in the books in accordance with PPSAS No. 17.
observations.reason:
Finding first covered in 2010 AAR


Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 3

observations.recommendation:
We recommended  that the Municipal Accountant: 
 regularly provide data to the Provincial Accountant on the Province’s share in the real property and other tax collections made by the Municipality and ensure reconciliation of the balances at year-end; 
submit details of the recorded balances of fund transfers for those payables recorded in 2016 and prior years; and, 
follow up recording of liquidation reports to ensure that these are recorded by the Provincial Accountant and immediately submit liquidation documents for those unliquidated fund transfers recorded as Due from LGUs in the Province’s books.
observations.reason:
Finding first covered in 2014 AAR

Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 4

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to conduct complete physical count of all PPEs of the Municipality and report the same per type/classification/category in the RPCPPE, in accordance with the above mentioned guidelines. It is also recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee agreed to regularly reconcile the PPE accounts between the accounting and inventory records.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to compute and record in the books, depreciation expenses of these PPEs, using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to transfer the cost of the completed projects from the Trust Fund to the General Fund and provide depreciation expenses accordingly.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
continuously provide data to the Provincial Accountant on the Province’s share in the real property and other tax collections made by the Municipality and ensure reconciliation of the balances at year-end;
submit details of the recorded balances of fund transfers for those payables recorded in prior years; and
follow up the recording of liquidation reports to ensure that these are recorded by the Provincial Accountant and immediately submit liquidation documents for those unliquidated fund transfers recorded as Due from LGUs in the Province's books.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that all POs include the brand names and complete detailed specification of the items being procured, in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010. Moreover, we recommended and Management agreed that for procurement of catering services, the complete detailed list of food items to be served is indicated in the Purchase Request, Request for Price Quotation and Purchase Order.

It was also recommended that the Inspectorate Committee and the end-user ensure that goods delivered are as specified/ indicated in the POs/Contracts.

We recommended and the Bids and Awards Committee agreed to ensure that only the relevant specifications and performance requirements of the goods/materials to be purchased are indicated in the PR and Invitation to Bids without reference to brand names.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to properly prepare and maintain cashbooks in accordance with the provisions of Sections 26 and 27 of the MNGAS for LGUs.  These cashbooks should be updated daily and reconciled with the accounting records at least quarterly. Necessary corrections, if any, shall be effected immediately.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce the provisions specified in the GAA on the net take home pay to promote efficiency and effectiveness in public service by ensuring that government employees have enough to support their daily needs.

We recommended and the LDRRM Council and the Municipal DRRM Officer agreed to ensure that the annual LDRRMWFP include the projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the discrepancies noted be communicated to the suppliers to demand correction/replacement of the items. Subsequently, all deliveries must be thoroughly inspected by the Local Inspectorate Team to ascertain that all the specifications provided in the purchase order/contract had been met.  Any deviations or variations from the purchase agreement shall be immediately communicated to the supplier for correction.

We recommended that Management: 
require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the above-mentioned development projects; 
direct the Municipal Development Council and the implementing offices to ensure that only projects which are feasible and can be completed within the year are included in the Annual Development Plan; and, 
ensure that the implementing offices are provided with adequate manpower and skills in the implementation of the 20% DF projects.
We recommended that Management implement the planned programs and projects within the approved implementation schedule so the constituents can immediately enjoy the intended benefits thereof.

We recommended that:  
the Municipal Engineer submit all the documents of the completed projects such as the Report on Completed Projects, Certificate of Project Completion and Inspection and Acceptance Report to the Municipal Accountant to support the transfer of CIP to appropriate asset accounts; and, 

the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee analyze and reconcile the PPEs between accounting records and RPCPE and draw the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended that the Disposal Committee expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386. In addition, the Municipal Accountant shall reclassify the cost of the unserviceable PPEs to Other Assets account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:     

initiate request for write-off, in the event that supporting documents could not be retrieved, in accordance with the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005; 
conduct regular verification, analysis and validation of the receivables and fund transfers made to facilitate the timely collectability and liquidation/ settlement of the same; and 
submit liquidation reports to settle account Due to other Funds to close the account.

We recommended that the unserviceable bulldozer be properly kept in a manner which will minimize, if not prevent, its exposure to elements and further deterioration, in compliance with Section 2 of P.D. 1445. 

Also, we recommended that Management update evaluation/ inspection of the unserviceable equipment and if found beyond repair, consider other ways to utilize the same or consider disposal thereof through sale or transfer to recover even at least its salvage value.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee agreed to exert diligent efforts to reconcile the PPE accounts and thereafter the Municipal Accountant draw journal entry voucher (JEV) to adjust the accounts affected.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record the transfer of the cost of completed projects booked as PPE under the Trust Fund to the General Fund. Corresponding depreciation expenses shall then be provided in the books in accordance with PPSAS No. 17.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
regularly provide data to the Provincial Accountant on the Province’s share in the real property and other tax collections made by the Municipality and ensure reconciliation of the balances at year-end;
submit details of the recorded balances of fund transfers for those payables recorded in 2016 and prior years; and,
follow up recording of liquidation reports to ensure that these are recorded by the Provincial Accountant and immediately submit liquidation documents for those unliquidated fund transfers recorded as Due from LGUs in the Province’s books.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
the Municipal Engineer submit all the documents of the completed projects such as the Report on Completed Projects, Certificate of Project Completion and Inspection and Acceptance Report to the Municipal Accountant to support the transfer of CIP to appropriate asset accounts; and,
the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee analyze and reconcile the PPEs between accounting records and RPCPE and draw the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide the Appraisal Committee with the list of PPEs acquired in 2002 and earlier so that the latter could appraise and determine their fair market value and estimated remaining useful lives. The Municipal Accountant shall then compute the depreciation expenses using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method, and record the same in the books.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
initiate request for write-off, in the event that supporting documents could not be retrieved, in accordance with the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005;
conduct regular verification, analysis and validation of the receivables and fund transfers made to facilitate the timely collectability and liquidation/settlement of the same; and,
submit liquidation reports to settle account Due to other Funds to close the account.
We recommended and the Disposal Committee agreed to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended and Management agreed to communicate the discrepancies noted to the suppliers and demand correction/replacement of the items. Subsequently, all deliveries must be thoroughly inspected by the Local Inspectorate Team to ascertain that all the specifications provided in the purchase order/contract had been met.  Any deviations or variations from the purchase agreement shall be immediately communicated to the supplier for correction.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the above-mentioned development projects;
direct the Municipal Development Council and the implementing offices to ensure that only projects which are feasible and can be completed within the year are included in the Annual Development Plan; and,
ensure that the implementing offices are provided with adequate manpower and skills in the implementation of the 20% DF projects.

We recommended and Management agreed to implement the planned programs and projects within the approved implementation schedule so the constituents can immediately enjoy the intended benefits thereof.

We recommended and Management agreed that the unserviceable bulldozer be properly kept in a manner which will minimize, if not prevent, its exposure to elements and further deterioration, in compliance with Section 2 of P.D. 1445.
Also, we recommended and Management agreed to update evaluation/inspection of the unserviceable equipment and if found beyond repair, consider other ways to utilize the same or consider disposal thereof through sale or transfer to recover even at least its salvage value.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to:
validate all records available by conducting actual physical count per department;
coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer in reconciling the RPCPPE with the accounting records; and
subsequently, furnish the Municipal Accountant with a copy of reconciled RPCPPE.

We recommended and Management agreed that: 
the specific estimated useful life for each PPE be determined based on the LGU’s experience on the life of its PPE; and

the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation expenses using the determined estimated useful lives and record the same in the books.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
the Municipal Engineer facilitate retrieval of reports/records pertaining to completed projects in CY 2014 and prior years and furnish the Municipal Accountant copies thereof; and

the Municipal Accountant use as basis the retrieved reports/records furnished by the Municipal Engineer in the reconciliation of prior years’ balances of CIP accounts and facilitate the immediate reclassification to proper PPE accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed that a Disposal Committee be immediately created to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable properties to prevent their further deterioration and reduction of salable value resulting to additional loss for the Municipality. In addition, these unserviceable properties be turned over to the Property Officer and their corresponding Memorandum of Receipt or Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment be cancelled.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
the salaries of the Accountable Officers with overdue cash advances be withheld until the same are settled/liquidated; 

demand letters be sent to the AOs who are no longer connected with the municipality and institute legal actions, if warranted. In cases of deceased employees, write-off of unliquidated cash advances be initiated in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and

for the cash advance of the Cashier granted in 2006, look into the application of the money value of her terminal leave to her accountability.

We recommended and Management agreed that all succeeding procurement be done through competitive bidding as required by the Government Procurement Act, except on circumstances where the alternative modes of procurement may be allowed.
We recommended and Management has agreed to increase the amount of bond of the Accountable Officer to ensure the maximum coverage of accountability in case of loss.

We recommended and Management agreed to: 

require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the on-going development projects, namely: 1) Construction of Multi-purpose building, 2) Development of potential water sources, 3) Infrastructure projects to schools and barangays, and 4) Acquisition of lot for informal settlers;  

direct the Municipal Development Council to ensure that the development projects identified in the ADP are feasible and can be implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation; and,

require the implementation of the development projects within the time schedule as targeted in the ADP and optimally utilize the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

the bulldozer be subjected to an evaluation/inspection in order to determine its current physical condition whether the possibility of being repaired is still viable and economical; and 

if repair is viable and economical, allocate budget for the immediate repair of the bulldozer.

We recommended and Management agreed to refrain from expending the QRF in the absence of a Presidential or local sanggunian declaration of a state of calamity to conserve the funds intended for use in the event of calamities and disasters.

We recommended and the MCPC agreed to:
articulate properly the PPAs and their objectives along with expected results so that only programs aligned with those specified in the Circular are planned;

ensure that the planned PPAs are implemented within the timeline set; and

measure results of implemented PPAs and submit a report thereon to the concerned Offices including COA Audit Team for proper evaluation.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
the salaries of the Accountable Officers with overdue cash advances be withheld until the same are settled/liquidated; 

demand letters be sent to the AOs who are no longer employed at the municipality and institute legal actions, if warranted. In cases of deceased employees, write-off of unliquidated cash advances be initiated in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and

for the cash advance of the Cashier granted in 2006, look into the application of the money value of her terminal leave to her accountability.

We recommended and management agreed that:
a Disposal Committee be created;
b.   pertinent documents be prepared by the Disposal Committee to     facilitate appraisal review of the unserviceable properties by the COA, including:
a properly accomplished Inventory and Inspection report for Unserviceable Properties,
Appraisal Report of unserviceable properties for disposal,     
other related documents such as photographs of unserviceable properties for disposal; and, 
file the application for disposal with the COA, pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.reason: Difficulty in retrieving previous years’ records

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Supply Officer agreed that in view of the provided appropriation, immediate repair of the bulldozer be done so that it can be utilized for the implementation of infrastructure projects of the Municipality and barangays, thus, the constituents can avail of the intended benefits for the purchase thereof.

We recommended and the Municipal Development Council through the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator agreed that the 20% DF be optimally utilized and the council identify and include in the development plan only the projects that are feasible and can be easily implemented by the Municipality within the year considering its limited technical manpower.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to:
validate all records available by conducting actual physical count per department;
coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer in reconciling the RPCPPE with the accounting records; and
subsequently, furnish the Municipal Accountant with a copy of reconciled RPCPPE.

We further recommended and it was agreed that the Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee agree on similar and uniform classification and recording of PPEs. Likewise, disposed properties be properly dropped from the books.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
the specific estimated useful life for each PPE be determined based on the LGU’s experience on the life of its PPE; and
the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation expenses using the determined estimated useful lives and record the same in the books.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive create an Appraisal Committee to estimate the current fair value and useful lives of the PPEs based on the reconciled RPCPPE, which shall then serve as the basis of the Municipal Accountant in the computation of their depreciation.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
the Municipal Engineer facilitate retrieval of reports/records pertaining to completed projects in CY 2014 and prior years and furnish the Municipal Accountant copies thereof; and
the Municipal Accountant use as basis the retrieved reports/records furnished by the Municipal Engineer in the reconciliation of prior years’ balances of CIP accounts and facilitate the immediate reclassification to proper PPE accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed that a Disposal Committee be immediately created to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable properties to prevent their further deterioration and reduction of salable value resulting to additional loss for the Municipality. In addition, these unserviceable properties be turned over to the Property Officer and their corresponding Memorandum of Receipt or Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment be cancelled.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
the salaries of the Accountable Officers with overdue cash advances be withheld until the same are settled/liquidated;
demand letters be sent to the AOs who are no longer connected with the municipality and institute legal actions, if warranted. In cases of deceased employees, write-off of unliquidated cash advances be initiated in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and
for the cash advance of the Cashier granted in 2006, look into the application of the money value of her terminal leave to her accountability.

We recommended and Management agreed that all succeeding procurement be done through competitive bidding as required by the Government Procurement Act, except on circumstances where the alternative modes of procurement may be allowed.
We recommended and Management has agreed to increase the amount of bond of the Accountable Officer to ensure the maximum coverage of accountability in case of loss.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
require the implementing offices to expedite the completion of the on-going development projects, namely: 1) Construction of Multi-purpose building, 2) Development of potential water sources, 3) Infrastructure projects to schools and barangays, and 4) Acquisition of lot for informal settlers;
direct the Municipal Development Council to ensure that the development projects identified in the ADP are feasible and can be implemented within the year with the proposed budget allocation; and,
require the implementation of the development projects within the time schedule as targeted in the ADP and optimally utilize the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
the bulldozer be subjected to an evaluation/inspection in order to determine its current physical condition whether the possibility of being repaired is still viable and economical; and
if repair is viable and economical, allocate budget for the immediate repair of the bulldozer.

We recommended and Management agreed to refrain from expending the QRF in the absence of a Presidential or local sanggunian declaration of a state of calamity to conserve the funds intended for use in the event of calamities and disasters.

We recommended and the MCPC agreed to:
articulate properly the PPAs and their objectives along with expected results so that only programs aligned with those specified in the Circular are planned;
ensure that the planned PPAs are implemented within the timeline set; and
measure results of implemented PPAs and submit a report thereon to the concerned Offices including COA Audit Team for proper evaluation.

We recommended that an Appraisal Committee be created to appraise and provide values for the remaining useful lives of the PPE to serve as basis by the Municipal Accountant in the computation of depreciation, in accordance with the provisions of Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer agreed to make proper coordination in the retrieving documents/reports which will provide information on the status of the projects comprising the CIP accounts. In addition, the Municipal Engineer agreed to furnish the Municipal Accountant with quarterly Report on Completed Projects so the latter could book up the same in the appropriate asset accounts.

We recommended and the BAC agreed that during preliminary examination of bids, they should ensure that bid tenders submitted by the bidders will include, among others, brand names of goods in the Technical Specifications. Also, upon award, the same information shall be embodied in the Contract of Agreement/Purchase Order between the Municipality and the winning bidder, as prescribed in COA Circular No. 96-010. Moreover, bid evaluation shall be in accordance with the procedures prescribed in Section 30 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended and Management agreed to prioritize the construction of a municipal garage/motor pool to shelter its vehicles and heavy equipment and protect them against the elements of nature as well as to safeguard the same against damage due to vandalism and possible theft or cannibalization of parts.

We recommended and Management agreed that the prescribed minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay required under the GAA be enforced immediately to promote efficiency and effectiveness in delivering public service by ensuring that government employees have enough to support their daily needs. It was also agreed that only those loan applications of employee-borrowers with paying capacity above the minimum mandated net take home pay be approved.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the information and details of the unexpended DRRMF in accordance with the reporting guidelines set in COA Circular Nos. 2012-002 and 2014-002.

We recommended and the GAD focal person agreed to submit the Annual GPB to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval thru the Provincial Government and furnish the COA Audit Team the approved GPB. At the end of the year, Accomplishment Report shall likewise be furnished to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to have an appraisal for all properties without records as to the remaining estimated useful life and value. Likewise, a timeline be set for the completion of this activity within the year.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee agreed to reconcile the differences in their records. Subsequently, draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to adjust the account balances in the books of accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office to retrieve documents and other essential information on the status of projects comprising the CIP accounts and set a reasonable target for the reclassification of completed projects to their appropriate asset and Infrastructure Asset accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to expedite the retrieval of files/records to determine their composition, nature, existence and validity for the preparation of adjusting entries, if warranted, to arrive at reliable balances.

We recommended and management agreed that:
The salaries of employees whose cash advances as of December 31, 2011 remained unliquidated be suspended, despite final demand pursuant to Section 9.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-004 and withheld the salaries of those who have unliquidated cash advances granted in CY 2015 in accordance with Section 5.9 of COA Circular 97-002.
 
In cases of deceased employees, write-off of unliquidated cash advances be initiated in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-001 dated February 5, 1997; and
The Municipal Accountant avoid granting additional cash advances prior to liquidation of previous cash advances.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Effect immediately the remittances to the Province of Guimaras, its shares in the real property tax collections made by the Municipality;
Trace the details of the fund transfers and submit liquidations to the Province of Guimaras; and,

Conduct periodic reconciliation with the Provincial Accountant.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to immediately enforce the prescribed minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay required under the GAA to promote efficiency and effectiveness in delivering public service by ensuring that government employees have enough to support their daily needs.
We also recommended that the paying capacity of each employee- borrower above the minimum mandated net take home pay be considered prior to the approval of loan applications.

We recommended and management agreed that:
The Supply Officer send a follow-up letter to the supplier to hasten the repair of the defective part of the bulldozer;
Doable solutions/options be immediately provided to utilize the bulldozer; and
A feasibility study or cost analysis on the selected solutions/options be made to determine which is more beneficial to the Municipality.

We recommended and management agreed that proper planning and scheduling be made in the formulation of annual development plan and that the Municipal Development Council set realistic number of projects with due consideration of the Municipality’s manpower resources and unexpected events that could affect the implementation of the planned projects.

We recommended and management agreed that:
The GAD focal person submit the Annual GPB to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval thru the Provincial Government and furnish the COA Audit Team the approved GPB.
The GAD Focal Person submit a copy of the Accomplishment Report for CY 2015 to the Audit Team and henceforth, submit the same within the prescribed period.
The structure and composition of the GFPS be changed to conform with Section 4.1(2) of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in this Report
Finding No. 14 | The Plan and Accomplishment Report submitted were nit duly received and approved by DILG

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that:

the salaries of the Accountable Officers with overdue cash advances be withheld until the same are settled/liquidated;
demand letters be sent to the AOs who are no longer employed at the municipality and institute legal actions, if warranted. In cases of deceased employees, write-off of unliquidated cash advances be initiated in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and
for the cash advance of the Cashier granted in 2006, look into the application of the money value of her terminal leave to her accountability.

We recommended that an Appraisal Committee be created to appraise and provide values for the remaining useful lives of the PPE to serve as basis by the Municipal Accountant in the computation of depreciation, in accordance with the provisions of Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards.

We recommended and management agreed that:
a Disposal Committee be created;
pertinent documents be prepared by the Disposal Committee to     facilitate appraisal review of the unserviceable properties by the COA, including:
a properly accomplished Inventory and Inspection report for Unserviceable Properties,
Appraisal Report of unserviceable properties for disposal,
other related documents such as photographs of unserviceable properties for disposal; and,
file the application for disposal with the COA, pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer agreed to make proper coordination in the retrieving documents/reports which will provide information on the status of the projects comprising the CIP accounts. In addition, the Municipal Engineer agreed to furnish the Municipal Accountant with quarterly Report on Completed Projects so the latter could book up the same in the appropriate asset accounts.

We recommended and the BAC agreed that during preliminary examination of bids, they should ensure that bid tenders submitted by the bidders will include, among others, brand names of goods in the Technical Specifications. Also, upon award, the same information shall be embodied in the Contract of Agreement/Purchase Order between the Municipality and the winning bidder, as prescribed in COA Circular No. 96-010. Moreover, bid evaluation shall be in accordance with the procedures prescribed in Section 30 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended and Management agreed to prioritize the construction of a municipal garage/motor pool to shelter its vehicles and heavy equipment and protect them against the elements of nature as well as to safeguard the same against damage due to vandalism and possible theft or cannibalization of parts.

During the exit conference, the Supply Officer commented that construction of motorpool has begun recently, and its target date of completion is July 2017.

We recommended and Management agreed that the prescribed minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay required under the GAA be enforced immediately to promote efficiency and effectiveness in delivering public service by ensuring that government employees have enough to support their daily needs. It was also agreed that only those loan applications of employee-borrowers with paying capacity above the minimum mandated net take home pay be approved.

We recommended and the Supply Officer agreed that in view of the provided appropriation, immediate repair of the bulldozer be done so that it can be utilized for the implementation of infrastructure projects of the Municipality and barangays, thus, the constituents can avail of the intended benefits for the purchase thereof.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the information and details of the unexpended DRRMF in accordance with the reporting guidelines set in COA Circular Nos. 2012-002 and 2014-002.

We recommended and the Municipal Development Council through the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator agreed that the 20% DF be optimally utilized and the council identify and include in the development plan only the projects that are feasible and can be easily implemented by the Municipality within the year considering its limited technical manpower.

We recommended and the GAD focal person agreed to submit the Annual GPB to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval thru the Provincial Government and furnish the COA Audit Team the approved GPB. At the end of the year, Accomplishment Report shall likewise be furnished to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to conduct a complete physical count of all the property, plant and equipment owned by the Municipality every year and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to comply with the prescribed guidelines under RA 7305 for the payment of hazard pay.

We recommended the following measures:

a. That the MDRRM Coordinator and the Municipal Accountant ensure that only expenditures directly related to disaster risk management activities shall be charged against the 5% MDRRMF.
b. That a separate budget for the Office of the MDRRM be prepared to finance their activities and supplies needed.

We recommended and management agreed to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 272 of RA No. 7160 as implemented by DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 dated April 14, 1998, No. 01-A dated March 14, 2000 and No. 01-B dated June 25, 2001 in the utilization of SEF.
